title: An LLM System for Autonomous Variational Quantum Circuit Design
authors: Kenya Sakka, Wataru Mizukami, Kosuke Mitarai
abstract: The design of high performing quantum circuits remains largely dependent on human expertise. We introduce an autonomous agentic framework that employs large language models (LLMs) to conduct iterative quantum circuit designs under explicit design constraints. Our system integrates seven components: Exploration, Generation, Discussion, Validation, Storage, Evaluation, and Review. These components form a closed-loop workflow that combines web-based knowledge acquisition, literature-grounded critique, executable code generation, and experimental feedback. We evaluate the framework on two tasks: quantum feature map construction for quantum machine learning and ansatz generation for variational quantum eigensolver applications in quantum chemistry. In image classification benchmarks, the best generated feature map outperforms representative quantum feature maps and, when scaled to larger qubit counts, surpasses the classical radial basis function kernel. In molecular ground state estimation across seven molecules, the generated ansatz attains competitive accuracy with widely used chemically inspired and hardware-efficient constructions while satisfying the imposed scaling constraints. These results establish LLM driven agentic system as a viable paradigm for automated quantum circuit design and illustrate how AI systems can participate in iterative scientific optimization workflows across scientific domains.
summary: The paper introduces an LLM-driven agentic framework designed to autonomously design complex quantum circuits for tasks like QML and VQE. By integrating web search, expert critique, code generation, and validation in a closed loop, the system demonstrates superior performance compared to manual or task-specific methods. This establishes AI as a viable paradigm for automated scientific discovery in quantum computing.
main_problem: The design of high-performing quantum circuits, required for tasks like QML feature mapping or VQE ansatz generation, currently relies heavily on specialized human expertise.
main_result: An autonomous agentic framework utilizing LLMs was demonstrated to iteratively and systematically design quantum circuits that achieve state-of-the-art performance compared to existing methods.
method: The system employs a closed-loop workflow involving seven components (Exploration, Generation, Discussion, Validation, Evaluation, Storage, Review) powered by LLMs for iterative refinement of circuit designs.
model_or_system: The work focuses on designing quantum circuits (ansätze or feature maps) parameterized by angles ($	heta$) to prepare quantum states $|\psi(	heta)angle$ used in variational algorithms like VQE. The underlying physics involves molecular Hamiltonians and qubit representations.
key_observables: Image classification accuracy (for QML); Ground state energy estimation error relative to chemical accuracy thresholds (for VQE).
important_parameters: Qubit count ($N$), number of trainable parameters ($	ext{n\_params}$), CNOT counts, and circuit depth/complexity.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes LLMs can effectively manage complex scientific reasoning across multiple iterative steps; specific technical limitations include restricting methods like ADAPT-VQE due to complexity constraints.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the seven-component agentic workflow, performance comparisons (e.g., feature map vs. RBF kernel), and scaling analysis of circuit parameters/errors versus qubit count or module inclusion.
paper_structure: The paper details the architecture of the autonomous agentic system, applying it first to QML feature maps and then expanding its scope to generate complex, physically motivated ansätze for VQE in quantum chemistry.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the bottleneck of human expertise in designing optimal quantum circuits by proposing a general-purpose AI paradigm that can automate scientific optimization workflows across different physical domains.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Overview of the proposed agentic system for automatic quantum circuit generation. The system autonomously conducts experiments and refines circuit designs through iterative collaboration among its components. The process consists of seven components: “Exploration“, “Generation”, “Discussion”, “Validation”, “Evaluation”, “Storage”, and “Review”. The gray-shaded areas indicate the stages that require human involvement, such as selecting task type, and optionally defining constraints, whereas the other parts operate fully automatically through computer-based processes. The green-shaded regions denote modules powered by LLMs.

FIG. 2: The performance trajectories of the quantum feature map and ansatz over 15 trials. The gray-shaded region represents the exploration phase, during which diverse candidates were generated to explore various directions inspired by the research report. The red crosses denote the raw performance values in each trial, while the red circles indicate the best values obtained up to that trial. The upper panel shows the SVM classification results obtained using the generated quantum feature map, and the lower panel shows the VQE results using the generated ansatz.

FIG. 3: Evaluation of the scalability of the best-performing quantum feature map generated by the agentic system. The x-axis represents the number of qubits. The y-axis indicates the classification accuracy on the test datasets. The dotted line represents the mean accuracy of the RBF kernel, and the star markers indicate cases where the quantum feature map outperform the RBF kernel.

FIG. 5: Scalability analysis of the well-known and our best ans¨atze. The scaling of the number of trainable parameters (left) and the gate counts (right) with respect to qubit count. The gate counts were evaluated after decomposing the circuits into the gate set {RX, RY , RZ, CNOT}

FIG. 4: Ground-state potential energy curves (PECs) estimated by VQE (left) and energy errors relative to exact energy (FCI or CASCI) (right). The right panel plots the errors for several well-known ans¨atze as well as for the best-performing ansatz generated by our system. From top to bottom, the plots correspond to H2, H4, H6, LiH, BeH2, H2O and N2 molecules. The green shaded area indicates the threshold for chemical accuracy.
